MEL.summary: Melanoma is a potentially aggressive skin cancer arising from melanocytes. Early detection and biopsy of suspicious lesions improves outcomes.
MEL.triage: Refer to dermatology for dermoscopy and possible biopsy when lesion shows asymmetry, border irregularity, color variegation, diameter growth, or evolving features.

BCC.summary: Basal cell carcinoma is a common skin cancer that is locally invasive but rarely metastasizes. Clinical assessment and histopathology confirm diagnosis.
BCC.triage: Recommend dermatology evaluation; treatment often involves local excision or topical/ablative therapies.

SCC.summary: Squamous cell carcinoma can be more aggressive than BCC and has a higher risk of metastasis. Lesions that are ulcerated, rapidly growing, or symptomatic require prompt evaluation.
SCC.triage: Urgent dermatology review recommended for suspicious features; biopsy often indicated.

NV.summary: Common benign melanocytic nevi (moles). Most are benign but changes over time can signal concern.
NV.triage: Self-monitoring and periodic photography; seek clinician review for evolving or symptomatic lesions.

AKIEC.summary: Actinic keratoses are sun-damaged precancerous lesions; some may progress to squamous cell carcinoma.
AKIEC.triage: Dermatology assessment recommended for suspicious or persistent lesions; consider treatment to reduce progression risk.

BKL.summary: Seborrheic keratoses are benign epidermal tumors; typically harmless but may be removed for symptoms or diagnostic uncertainty.
BKL.triage: Generally benign; consider clinician review if changing or symptomatic.

DF.summary: Dermatofibromas are common benign fibrous nodules; usually harmless and monitored clinically.
DF.triage: Monitor clinically; refer if atypical features or patient concern.

VASC.summary: Vascular lesions include hemangiomas and angiomas; most are benign but may require evaluation if changing or symptomatic.
VASC.triage: Clinical review advised for evolving or symptomatic vascular lesions.
